hints: For a polite request not to do something, we use "would you mind".

a1.would: The phrase "would you mind" is a standard way to politely ask someone to stop or not do something.

hints: To express ability or skill, we use the modal verb "can".

a1.can: "Can" expresses the ability or skill to do something, in this case - the ability to speak six languages.

hints: For supposition or possibility, we use "may" or "might".

a1.may: "May" and "might" express possibility or supposition that someone may be ill.

hints: To make a polite request, you can use "could", "can" or "will".

a1.could: In polite requests, you can use "Could you", "Can you" or "Will you please".

hints: To express advice or recommendations about the past, we use "should have" or "ought to have".

a1.should: "Should have told" or "ought to have told" expresses that it would have been right to tell the truth, but it wasn't done.

a1.mustn't: "Mustn't" expresses a strict prohibition - you cannot bring animals into England.

a1.can't: "Can't move" means that the piano is so heavy that it cannot be moved.

hints: To make a suggestion or request for action, we use "shall".

a1.shall: "Shall we" is used to suggest doing something together.

hints: To express the impossibility of doing something, we use "can't".

a1.can't: "Can't" shows that a person cannot go along with someone.

hints: To express advice or recommendation, we use "shouldn't".

a1.shouldn't: "Shouldn't" expresses advice not to eat so many sweets.

hints: To express necessity or obligation, we use "must".

a1.must: "Must" expresses the necessity or strong desire to buy a new dress and shoes.

hints: For a very polite request, we use "could".

a1.could: "I was wondering if I could" is a very polite way to ask for a favor.
